narration.default: Imagine you have to check ONE variable against MANY specific values — like a menu choice (1, 2, 3, 4, or 5), a grade letter (A, B, C, D, F), a key press (W, A, S, D for movement). You COULD write a long else-if chain (if grade == 'A', else if grade == 'B', else if grade == 'C', ...) but it gets repetitive fast. C has a cleaner tool for this exact case: the switch statement. Same logic, less typing, and it tells the reader "I am picking from a list of fixed values" at a glance. switch is your friend whenever you have ONE value with MANY possible specific cases.

Line 4: char grade = 'B'; — single quotes around B because grade is a char (one character). The switch coming next will look at this variable and pick a CASE based on what it holds. switch only works with whole-number-like types (int, char, enum) — not strings or floats. char counts because each character is internally a small number (A is 65, B is 66, etc.).

Lines 5 to 17 are the switch block. The structure is: switch (variable) { case VALUE: do stuff; break; case ANOTHER_VALUE: do stuff; break; default: do fallback; }. Think of it like a vending machine: the switch looks at the input (grade), checks each case from top to bottom for a match ('A' first? 'B' second? 'C' third?), and runs the matching case. If nothing matches, default runs (like the else at the end of an if-else chain).

Notice the break; line at the end of each case. break tells C "I am done with this case, EXIT the switch entirely". Without break, C "falls through" to the NEXT case and runs that too — and the one after that. Forgetting break is one of the most common switch bugs. Try removing the break after case 'A' (delete line 8) and run with grade = 'A' — you'll see "Excellent!" AND "Good job." AND "Keep trying." all print. That fall-through behavior is sometimes useful but usually a bug. Always end each case with break unless you really mean fall-through.

Trace it: grade is 'B'. switch checks case 'A' — no match. Checks case 'B' — MATCH. Runs the body: prints "Good job." then break exits the switch entirely. The default case is skipped. Click Run to confirm — Console shows "Good job." Now change grade to 'A' and rerun → "Excellent!" Try 'C', then 'X' (unknown), then 'F' (unknown). The default catches anything that doesn't match any specific case.
